--- SOURCE 1: Guide to Data Center Cooling Technologies ---
URL: https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/future-of-data-center-cooling

SUMMARY:
articles Image 14: breadcrumb-arrowThe Future of Data Center Cooling: Innovations for Sustainability Image 16: template-related From traditional air cooling to liquid cooling to hybrid technologies, each has the power to help the global data center ecosystem reduce its energy and water consumption when strategically applied to the right situation. Whether it’s AI, IoT, or HPC, our facilities are shell-ready and design-ready to meet the advanced computing cooling needs of our customers while offering sustainable solutions that lead the global data center ecosystem toward a lower carbon footprint, energy efficiency, and water conservation. Advanced cooling technologies — such as air-based systems that rely on chilled air circulation to remove heat or liquid cooling solutions that transfer heat away using coolant — are both efficient methods used to cool servers in data centers.

Article
The Future of Data Center Cooling: Innovations for Sustainability
=================================================================

[![Image 20: twitter](https://www.digitalrealty.com/images/twitter.svg)](https://twitter.com/share?text=The%20Future%20of%20Data%20Center%20Cooling:%20Innovations%20for%20Sustainability&url=www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/future-of-data-center-cooling?t=1749813347324&hashtags=digitalreality)[![Image 21: linkedin](https://www.digitalrealty.com/images/linkedin.svg)](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/future-of-data-center-cooling?t=1749813347324&title=The%20Future%20of%20Data%20Center%20Cooling:%20Innovations%20for%20Sustainability&source=Digital%20reality?latest)

**Kyle Chien**, Senior Director of Market Insights at Innovation Acceleration Group, Digital Realty

 March 7, 2025

As technology, internet traffic, and workloads require more computing resources to store, process, manage, and transfer data, the demand for [data centers](https://www.digitalrealty.com/data-centers) increases. A recent [S&P Global Ratings](https://www.spglobal.com/ratings/en/research/pdf-articles/241204-global-credit-outlook-2025-promise-and-peril-101609737) report forecasts data center capacity will increase at a compound annual growth rate of about 25%-30% to nearly $650 billion by 2028, from less than $200 billion in 2023.

With this increase comes the challenge of balancing highly dense workloads with sustainability ambitions. But improving energy efficiency presents opportunities for the data center industry for [advanced cooling innovation](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/advanced-cooling-innovation), particularly for the right partners with the right data center design expertise.

At the heart of this challenge lies two truths:

1.   There’s no one-size-fits-all approach to cooling, particularly for [high-density workloads](https://www.digitalrealty.com/platform-digital/colocation/high-density-colocation), like artificial intelligence (AI) and [high-performance computing (HPC)](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/principles-high-performance-compute), that may require specialized cooling. 
2.   Implementing efficient and sustainable cooling solutions is the truly innovative way forward. Data centers produce heat — there’s no way around it. However, the right data center partner for your deployment not only manages the amount of heat produced but also enables innovation through scalable and sustainable IT strategies that help IT leaders balance [sustainability](https://www.digitalrealty.com/about/esg) with business goals.

In this blog, we’ll:

*   Examine the range of cooling technologies currently employed within the industry. 
*   Dive into each method, providing use cases to highlight their advantages. 
*   Then, we’ll look at how innovative data center cooling can champion sustainability while addressing HPC demands. 

##### Why are data center cooling methods important?

To maintain optimal operating temperatures for HPC and dense server configurations, ensuring the reliability and performance of data center infrastructure is crucial. This includes deploying the right cooling technique for the appropriate hardware and scenario. Common methods include air cooling systems, liquid cooling solutions, and hybrid approaches that combine both techniques for maximum efficiency.

##### Data center cooling methods and systems

[Data Gravity](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/reports/data-gravity-index) is the idea that data has mass and as its size and importance grow, it becomes increasingly difficult to move or replicate. This means modern data centers increasingly host and interconnect a wide array of data, enabling everything from [financial transactions](https://www.digitalrealty.com/expertise/solutions/financial-services) to social media interactions.

As this demand increases, data centers grow in scale and complexity. And so does the importance of efficient cooling solutions, particularly for high-density workloads like AI and HPC. Let’s dig into some of the most prominent cooling solutions, highlighting their respective strengths through use cases.

###### Air cooling

Air cooling uses air conditioning, fans, and vents to circulate ambient air, expelling the hot air produced by computing equipment. It's the most traditional cooling method. 

 For less power-dense deployments, like AI inferencing, traditional air cooling may be right because machine learning (ML) requires fewer cooling resources. Air cooling also has widespread applicability, as data centers, small and large, can implement this cooling method. 

 Despite its broad applicability, air cooling is typically used in less than 20 kilowatts (kW) per rack in conventional designs, though it can go up to 35 kW in exceptional cases. Anything beyond that often requires liquid cooling (direct or immersion) and a combination (hybrid) approach. Digital Realty’s [high-density colocation](https://www.digitalrealty.com/platform-digital/colocation/high-density-colocation) solution can support up to 150 kW per rack.

###### Liquid cooling

Liquid cooling systems use coolants that circulate through a pipe network, absorbing heat away from IT equipment. This is especially effective for cooling high-density servers with AI and HPC workloads. 

 Take power-intensive workloads, such as training deployments, for example. These may require deep learning and generative AI (GenAI) and often require a larger environment to accommodate their size and complexity. By employing the conductive properties of liquids, the data center can quickly move heat away from the servers.

**Spotlight on our direct liquid cooling**

In 2023, Digital Realty launched a [cloud and carrier-neutral](https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/digital-realty-installs-direct-liquid-cooling-at-hpc-capable-data-center-in-paris-france/) data center in [Paris](https://www.digitalrealty.com/data-centers/emea/paris), emphasizing energy efficiency. [Addressing Data Gravity concerns](https://www.digitalrealty.com/platform-digital), the La Courneuve hub incorporates direct liquid cooling, focusing on performance needs while minimizing Scope 1 and 2 emissions. This innovative liquid cooling solution maximizes optimal energy use to align with our [sustainability objectives](https://www.digitalrealty.com/about/esg/sustainability-objectives) and brings economic advantages for partner enterprises.

###### Immersion cooling

[Immersion cooling](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/articles/the-data-center-of-the-future-spotlight-on-immersion-cooling) directly submerges IT infrastructure components in dielectric fluids that cool by absorbing heat. Servers, which are kept in nonconductive, absorbent fluids, provide effective heat management.

One of the biggest advantages of immersion cooling is that it enables the deployment of ultra-high-density equipment in otherwise low or medium-density facilities, essentially retrofitting the data center for future applications. High-density deployments, like AI and Internet of Things (IoT), are common use cases for the emerging immersion cooling.

###### Evaporative cooling

Evaporative cooling uses fans to draw outside air in, cooling it before deploying it to cool IT equipment. Through water evaporation, this method draws heat away from the air efficiently. 

 Depending on the specifics of the deployment, business use case, and location, evaporative cooling may be the right cooling technique. For example, as an alternative to traditional air cooling, and depending on other attributes, evaporative cooling may be appropriate for inferencing deployments.

###### Free cooling

[Cooling data centers using free air](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/white-papers/the-advantages-of-cooling-data-centres-using-free-air) bypasses traditional cooling mechanisms when external conditions make it possible. Free cooling capitalizes on external conditions, primarily cooler outside air or water, and typically employs several mechanisms to deliver, including economizers, indirect free air, thermal wheels, and plate-heat exchangers.

 While a site’s microclimate will need assessment to initially determine the viability of free cooling with a deployment, with viability ascertained, free air cooling can be one of a risk-adverse and energy-efficient solution for companies looking to minimize the carbon footprint of their data center deployments. This is especially relevant for AI and HPC deployment use cases.

###### Hybrid cooling strategy

A hybrid cooling approach integrates multiple cooling technologies to optimize thermal management based on specific IT equipment demands.

For example, a facility might combine traditional air cooling with rear-door heat exchangers, where the latter are mounted directly on server racks to absorb heat as it exits the servers. In this setup, ambient air cooling manages the overall rack environment while the rear-door heat exchanger efficiently handles high-density workloads by transferring excess heat (via liquid) away from critical components.

This blended strategy not only enhances energy efficiency and targeted cooling but also provides operational flexibility. Under favorable ambient conditions, additional methods such as evaporative or free cooling can further supplement the system, reducing reliance on mechanical chillers. By dynamically leveraging multiple cooling solutions, including the precision of rear-door heat exchangers, a hybrid deployment meets today’s cooling challenges and scales to future performance needs.

##### Data center cooling design and optimization

Data center cooling isn't only about installing fans and other coolants. It's about balancing design and optimization, as well as implementing efficiency and efficacy. Strategic design combined with optimization is crucial for ensuring that data center infrastructure and IT equipment operate at their best. This approach not only prevents IT equipment from overheating but also maximizes the efficient use of data center resources, such as power and cooling systems. By optimizing these elements, data centers can significantly reduce their environmental footprint, leading to more sustainable and cost-effective operations.

Below are some critical factors for optimizing data center performance:

*   **Configuring hot/cold aisle containment:** By configuring server racks in alternating rows — with cold air intakes facing one way and hot air output facing the other — data centers can create hot and cold “corridors.” This maximizes efficiency by ensuring cooled air is where it's needed most while preventing the hot and cold air from mixing. 
*   **Optimizing airflow management:** By optimizing airflow through vents and barriers, data centers can minimize areas of stationary air. Proper airflow management then lowers cooling costs by reducing the need to compensate for warm spots. It also helps ensure that the cooling is constant throughout the facility. 
*   **Working inside safe limits:**By adhering to the American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers' (ASHRAE) published guidance, data centers can find and implement [temperature standards](https://xp20.ashrae.org/datacom1_4th/ReferenceCard.pdf) for safe operating conditions of common equipment. In some cases, maintaining a slightly warmer temperature — when safe — can save considerable energy and costs. 
*   **Preventing downtime:**By leaning on IoT devices, ML, and automated systems for help, data centers can spot problems before they happen. Monitoring systems and installing timely patches and upgrades ensure facilities prevent downtime and improve efficiency further. 
*   **Managing humidity control:**By managing humidity, data centers can avoid hardware damage and damage to static electricity. 

###### Deploying liquid cooling: New facilities vs. retrofitting existing data centers

When evaluating data center options, it's critical to understand liquid cooling implementation and its impact on performance and reliability. In facilities designed from the ground up for liquid cooling, the initial build integrates dedicated coolant pathways, specialized server racks, and robust safety systems. This proactive design approach ensures the infrastructure is optimized for high thermal performance and energy efficiency from day one, offering a seamless, future-ready environment for your IT equipment.

Conversely, data centers that retrofit existing facilities to accommodate liquid cooling face a more complex challenge. These sites, originally built around air cooling systems, must undergo significant modifications — such as reconfiguring floor layouts, adjusting rack arrangements, and installing new coolant distribution units and leak detection systems — to support liquid cooling effectively. These upgrades can require a more intricate integration process, potentially impacting consistency during the transition.

Ultimately, when assessing a data center, it’s critical to consider the cooling infrastructure and the operator’s track record. Purpose-built facilities around liquid cooling from the outset are often more seamless in performance, and efficiency, but with a significant cost, while retrofitted sites can still be an excellent choice, when operated by a provider with [deep expertise](https://www.digitalrealty.com/expertise) and proven experience — such as Digital Realty. By selecting an established operator with a history of successfully adapting existing facilities, enterprises can maintain high levels of reliability and performance, even in environments initially engineered for air cooling.

##### Data center cooling trends and the future

Looking to the future of data center cooling, we see several exciting trends that can help further reduce the industry’s environmental impact. Using modern technologies and strategic [data center design](https://www.digitalrealty.com/data-centers/design), for example, could prove vital.

*   **Adaptive cooling:** Employing AI with cooling systems promises more adaptability than ever. These solutions continually learn and adjust to the specific cooling needs of data centers in real time — reducing overcooling and energy waste. 
*   **Edge data centers:** We could see even more data centers in strategic “edge” locations in the future. These edge centers ensure low-latency data and AI workload delivery and introduce innovative cooling possibilities in their unique settings. 
*   **Sustainable energy:**While it’s important to become more efficient at energy management, it’s also possible to use energy from renewable sources. Solar or wind-powered data centers, for example, are no longer a dream but a reality. Combining renewable energy sources and storage solutions, such as fuel cells and advanced batteries, with efficient cooling strategies presents a holistic approach to sustainability progress. This integrated approach ensures that data centers can operate reliably and efficiently while minimizing their environmental impact. By leveraging these technologies, data centers can achieve greater energy independence and reduce their carbon footprint.

**Spotlight on our cooling tower initiatives**

Digital Realty’s data centers are at the forefront of the industry in improving cooling efficiency. At our SIN10 facility in [Singapore](https://www.digitalrealty.com/data-centers/asia-pacific/singapore), for example, we employ DCI electrolysis for water preservation, saving 1.24 million liters monthly. This process eliminates chemical usage, allowing water usage for three times as long before becoming wastewater, lowering the water and power usage in Singapore.

##### Driving future data center cooling innovation sustainably

Various cooling systems exist in modern data centers, with each bringing unique benefits for specific deployment use cases. From traditional air cooling to liquid cooling to hybrid technologies, each has the power to help the global data center ecosystem reduce its energy and water consumption when strategically applied to the right situation. 

 Digital Realty understands there’s no one-size-fits-all approach to cooling and is committed to helping IT leaders transform their industries by implementing a core strategy that enables innovation while balancing sustainability with business goals. We do this by deploying innovative technologies in our data centers designed to meet the challenges of cooling increasing power-density workloads that aren’t just wholesale deployments but rather customer and deployment-specific. 

 Whether it’s AI, IoT, or HPC, our facilities are shell-ready and design-ready to meet the advanced computing cooling needs of our customers while offering sustainable solutions that lead the global data center ecosystem toward a lower carbon footprint, energy efficiency, and water conservation. 

 Learn more about deploying a future-ready IT infrastructure in your organization to meet your advanced computing cooling needs while balancing sustainability with your business goals in our white paper, [AI for IT Leaders](https://www.digitalrealty.com/resources/white-papers/ai-it-leaders). Then, feel free [to connect](https://www.digitalrealty.com/contact) with our experts to get started.

##### FAQs

**How is cooling done in a data center?**

Cooling in data centers occurs through various methods, like:

*   Air cooling 
*   Liquid cooling 
*   Immersion cooling 
*   Evaporative cooling 
*   Free cooling 
*   Hybrid cooling

IT leaders should choose a data center that tailors the cooling techniques based on several factors, including the workload density, hardware, and infrastructure requirements for a sustainable deployment. 

**What is the best cooling system for a data center?**

There’s no one-size-fits-all approach to data center cooling. Factors like the data center’s size, location, and workload demands, for example, all have an impact. Increasingly, sustainable and efficient solutions like immersion are becoming popular due to their higher efficiency and lower carbon footprint. 

**What are the cooling components of a data center?**

The key cooling components of a data center include:

*   Chillers 
*   Cooling towers 
*   Air conditioners (CRAC/CRAH) 
*   Liquid cooling pipes 
*   Cooling distribution units (CDU) 
*   Pumping systems 
*   Fans and vents 

Advanced AI monitoring systems for temperature and humidity control are also increasingly common as data centers become more sustainable. 

**How would you cool a server in a data center?**

Advanced cooling technologies — such as air-based systems that rely on chilled air circulation to remove heat or liquid cooling solutions that transfer heat away using coolant — are both efficient methods used to cool servers in data centers. Other methods include optimizing airflow through techniques like hot and cold aisle containment to prevent heat recirculation. For high-density servers, direct-to-chip liquid cooling or immersion cooling is ideal for maximum thermal efficiency and sustainability.

URL: https://www.datacenter-asia.com/industry-trends/data-center-cooling-solutions-in-2025-challenges-trends-and-innovations/

SUMMARY:
Data Center Cooling Solutions in 2025: Challenges, Trends, and Innovations - Data Center Asia Data Center Cooling Solutions in 2025: Challenges, Trends, and Innovations Without robust cooling systems, maintaining data center efficiency becomes a major challenge. This article explores the latest trends, challenges, and innovations in data center cooling solutions for 2025 and how these advancements are shaping the industry. Challenges in Data Center Cooling Solutions in 2025 As we move into 2025, data center cooling solutions face various challenges: Data center cooling solutions need to be more energy-efficient to address sustainability issues. Although advanced data center cooling solutions offer high efficiency, they often come with high initial costs. In 2025, low-cost data center cooling solutions are being prioritized.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: 2025-02-03T15:27:15+00:00

Data Center Cooling Solutions in 2025: Challenges, Trends, and Innovations
==========================================================================

In the digital era, data centers power our interconnected world. These critical facilities handle massive data processing tasks but generate significant heat as a result. Effective [data center cooling solutions](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/why-attend/)are essential to ensure smooth operations and prevent server overheating.

Without robust cooling systems, maintaining data center efficiency becomes a major challenge. This article explores the latest trends, challenges, and innovations in data center cooling solutions for 2025 and how these advancements are shaping the industry.

![Image 4](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Dca-feb-picPicture1.png)

Challenges in Data Center Cooling Solutions in 2025
---------------------------------------------------

With digitalization, the demand for data center cooling solutions is growing. The data center cooling market is experiencing high growth, estimated at USD 16.56 billion in 2024[1]. This growing demand is driven by the heightened workloads in modern data centers.

As we move into 2025, data center cooling solutions face various challenges:

**Increased Energy Demands**

The rise of AI workloads, edge computing, and hyperscale data centers has dramatically increased the strain on cooling solutions. These applications need vast computational power, resulting in more heat generation. To address this, data center cooling solutions must be upgraded to handle these high loads.

For that, data center cooling systems are designed to support the scaling of modern data centers. They ensure that servers remain within optimal temperature ranges to prevent downtime.

**Sustainability Pressures**

Data centers are major energy consumers whose environmental impact is becoming a key concern. Traditional cooling methods include air conditioning and cooling towers that consume more power.

Data center cooling solutions need to be more energy-efficient to address sustainability issues. By adopting eco-friendly systems, facilities can reduce their environmental impact and maintain operations.

**Cost Management**

Although advanced data center cooling solutions offer high efficiency, they often come with high initial costs. Balancing the investment with budget constraints is a major challenge.

To do so, companies must assess the long-term savings and operational benefits of these systems and manage their overall budgets.

![Image 5](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/DCA-febPicture2.png)

**New Cooling Trends for 2025**

The industry is committed to adopting innovative cooling mechanisms in response to the growing heat management challenges.

**Liquid Cooling Dominance**

Unlike traditional methods, liquid cooling data centers emerge as an effective solution. According to IDC analyst Sean Graham, 22% of data centers are using liquid cooling techniques[2]. These techniques involve direct-to-chip cooling, which directly transfers heat from equipment using coolants. These new technologies are more efficient than conventional processes, especially in high-density systems.

Also, [immersion cooling data center](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/why-exhibit/) systems are gaining popularity due to their eco-friendly approach. The process involves submerging servers in non-conductive liquid, which dissipates heat more efficiently. According to studies, immersion cooling can reduce energy usage by 50% compared to old air-cooling methods[3].

**AI-Powered Cooling:**

This year, data center operators are employing artificial intelligence for real-time optimization. AI algorithms can provide useful insights about temperature fluctuations, cooling inefficiencies, and more. This ensures that cooling resources are used only when needed.

Moreover, this approach also allows for early identification of potential issues. It helps to reduce downtime and minimize energy waste.

**Low-Cost Cooling System Improvements**

In 2025, low-cost data center cooling solutions are being prioritized. Simple adjustments and design innovations can deliver high performance at lower operational costs. For example, adjusting fan positions or sealing off unused areas can enhance cooling efficiency without any additional investment.

Also, technologies like free cooling that uses external natural cool air are optimized to reduce reliance on expensive, energy-hungry systems. Moreover, adopting liquid cooling data center methods contributes to cost reduction. As these systems require less energy, it’s also a cost-effective option in the long run.

**Higher Target Temperatures in Data Centers**

A rising trend in 2025 is allowing data centers to operate at higher target temperatures. Traditionally, server rooms were kept at temperatures in the low 70s°F. By increasing the threshold, facilities can achieve better energy efficiency and reduce cooling costs without compromising performance.

![Image 6](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/DCA-feb-picture3.png)

Why Attend Data Center Asia 2025
--------------------------------

Data center cooling solutions handle the rising demands of modern technology. As energy consumption rises, new approaches like liquid cooling, AI optimization, and sustainable practices are becoming key.

For anyone who wants to learn more about data center cooling solutions, [Data Center Asia 2025](https://www.datacenter-asia.com/) is a premier event in the Asia-Pacific region that you can’t miss. It will take place from July 15 to 17, 2025, at AsiaWorld Expo in Hong Kong, uniting industry leaders, innovators, and experts to explore the latest advancements in the data center industry.

FULL CONTENT:
By[Andrew Schaap](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/andrewschaap/ "https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/andrewschaap/")

# Here’s Why Data Center Cooling Is The Hottest Innovation In The Sector

![Forbes Technology Council](https://blogs-images.forbes.com/assets/images/avatars/blog-3949_400_6d18078e84d93600baaf18165a1394f8.jpg)

By[Andrew Schaap](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/andrewschaap/ "https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/andrewschaap/"),

Forbes Councils Member.

*Andrew Schaap is CEO and Board Member of* [*Aligned Data Centers*](https://aligneddc.com/ "https://aligneddc.com/")*.*

![ ](https://imageio.forbes.com/specials-images/imageserve/6601bb70850d28cc4e02a875//960x0.jpg?height=507&width=711&fit=bounds)

A team of scientists uses a supercomputer to zero in on the most effective [Covid vaccine](https://www.theregister.com/2022/03/22/pfizer_nvidia_ai/ "https://www.theregister.com/2022/03/22/pfizer_nvidia_ai/"). A busy executive saves time by prompting ChatGPT to draft an email. Taking stock of its contents, your smart fridge tells you what to pick up at the grocery store.

What do those three things have in common? They’re all powered by data centers, the [backbone of the digital world](https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbestechcouncil/2024/01/22/five-trends-driving-the-booming-data-center-economy-in-2024-and-why-investors-are-taking-notice/?sh=5c7d4ab76fa5 "https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbestechcouncil/2024/01/22/five-trends-driving-the-booming-data-center-economy-in-2024-and-why-investors-are-taking-notice/?sh=5c7d4ab76fa5").

They also generate heat and lots of it.

As a CEO in the rapidly growing data center industry, I’m excited by the massive demand that AI, machine learning and supercomputing are creating for our business. Besides making people’s lives easier and helping companies run better, there are many examples of how these advanced technologies are doing good for humanity.

But for data centers, they also pose a big challenge: how to keep all that hardware cool.

And what those unfamiliar with this space don’t know is that traditional cooling methods have started to outlive their usefulness. That’s putting data center providers in a race to create a cooling technology for the modern world—one that not only turns down the heat for high-performance computing but also uses energy more efficiently.

The sooner we can do that, the better. Demand for data centers will only keep growing, with their power consumption in the U.S. [projected to double](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/investing-in-the-rising-data-center-economy "https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/technology-media-and-telecommunications/our-insights/investing-in-the-rising-data-center-economy") between 2022 and 2030.

## Data Center Cooling 101

For data center providers, keeping things cool for customers is a priority. Doing that means responding to the laws of physics.

When electricity travels through a semiconductor, it creates heat. The more powerful the chip, the hotter it runs. Nvidia’s latest graphics processing unit (GPU)—which underpins many cutting-edge AI applications—contains a staggering [80 billion transistors](https://apnews.com/article/nvidia-chatgpt-ai-chips-stock-jump-9eee108170a799986c066f70829a7215 "https://apnews.com/article/nvidia-chatgpt-ai-chips-stock-jump-9eee108170a799986c066f70829a7215") that need cooling. It also uses as much energy as the [typical resident of a U.S. household](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/paul-churnock-pe-37877846_this-is-nvidias-h100-gpu-it-has-a-peak-activity-7142715284636774401-JC_I/ "https://www.linkedin.com/posts/paul-churnock-pe-37877846_this-is-nvidias-h100-gpu-it-has-a-peak-activity-7142715284636774401-JC_I/").

Pulling the resulting heat from a circuit board and its wiring is crucial to stop it from malfunctioning or even breaking down.

So far, the data center industry has mostly relied on air cooling to get the job done. This technology uses two sets of air ducts, bringing in air from outside and pushing it across the front of the equipment, then sending the warmed air to the back so it can be expelled.

The downsides? Besides consuming energy and water—needed to chill the machinery—the typical air-cooling system is bulky, taking up precious real estate. With its many moving parts, it’s also prone to breakdowns.

For data center providers, those sustainability and reliability concerns have prompted a race to develop alternatives to air cooling. The rise of GPUs and other hot-running hardware has only upped the ante over the past couple of years.

## Enter Liquid Cooling

Increasingly, data centers are turning to liquid cooling to meet their needs.

Before I continue, let’s be clear: In the data center world, there is no flux capacitor that will solve everyone’s cooling problems overnight. Until innovation in electricity takes a quantum leap with [superconductors](https://theconversation.com/scientists-have-been-researching-superconductors-for-over-a-century-but-they-have-yet-to-find-one-that-works-at-room-temperature-3-essential-reads-216806 "https://theconversation.com/scientists-have-been-researching-superconductors-for-over-a-century-but-they-have-yet-to-find-one-that-works-at-room-temperature-3-essential-reads-216806") that let computers operate at room temperature, we’ll have to make do with incremental improvements to existing technologies.

My money is on liquid cooling for one simple reason: Water is a better heat rejector than air, making it ideal for GPUs and other high-density computing hardware. Historically, the argument against liquid cooling has been that water shouldn’t be anywhere near electronic equipment. But the huge demand for [Nvidia and other AI chips](https://www.verdict.co.uk/nvidia-will-become-less-dominant-in-the-ai-chip-industry-globaldata/ "https://www.verdict.co.uk/nvidia-will-become-less-dominant-in-the-ai-chip-industry-globaldata/") has brushed those concerns aside.

Liquid cooling systems also use less power—and, perhaps surprisingly, less water—than their air-driven rivals. They have fewer moving parts, too. From a maintenance point of view, it’s like the difference between an electric vehicle and one with an internal combustion engine.

But no matter what happens, some air must flow at data centers. Most facilities will keep using air to cool equipment running at lower computing densities, and not all customers are keen to make dramatic changes.

## Why Liquid Is The Cooling Tech Of Tomorrow

Although the jury is still out, I believe liquid cooling is the future for data centers and will ultimately become the status quo.

Why? The business case is compelling. For customers and investors, liquid cooling means less capital expenditure for the same output. The payoff: Companies that depend on data centers will be able to crush the competition by making higher margins.

Switching to liquid cooling also means better water and power usage effectiveness (WUE and PUE), two key metrics in our industry. Compared to air cooling alone, it can shrink facility power by almost 20% and total data center power by 10%, a [recent study](https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/opinions/what-happens-when-you-introduce-liquid-cooling-into-an-air-cooled-data-center/ "https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/opinions/what-happens-when-you-introduce-liquid-cooling-into-an-air-cooled-data-center/") found.

The leap to liquid cooling will happen without anyone really noticing. As innovation sweeps the data center industry, legacy cooling equipment will inevitably be replaced. To me, it’s like the gradual shift from the old MD-80 passenger plane to the Dreamliner that is now an airline industry standard.

Helping drive that change is an enormous amount of infrastructure investment. From $250 billion in 2022, annual capital spending on data centers is forecast to surge to [more than $500 billion](https://www.silverliningsinfo.com/data-center/data-center-capex-poised-take-heres-how-itll-be-spent "https://www.silverliningsinfo.com/data-center/data-center-capex-poised-take-heres-how-itll-be-spent") by 2027. With investment in high-performance servers for AI set to grow at five times the outlay on their general-purpose counterparts, expect liquid cooling to take center stage.

Of course, for some, the transition to liquid cooling will mean overcoming a few hurdles. It can be capital-intensive, and there’s still a lack of standardization for this relatively new technology, along with a learning curve to make the shift.

But looking ahead, one thing’s for sure: Because AI and so many other industries rely on data centers, innovations that serve customers better will only keep gathering pace.

## Breadcrumb

# Cooling data centers: Keeping up with emerging technologies

Key factors data center operators need to consider when selecting a cooling method for their data center

> Explore Trendscape
>
> Our take on the interconnected global trends that are shaping the business climate for our clients.
>
> [Read more](https://www.whitecase.com/insights/trendscape "View our insights")

Explore Trendscape

Our take on the interconnected global trends that are shaping the business climate for our clients.

In today’s digital and hyperscale computing landscape, the need for data centers has grown exponentially. According to the International Data Corporation, the global data sphere is expected to grow to 175 zettabytes by 2025, up from 33 zettabytes in 2018, with no signs of abating. This growth in data center infrastructure has led to a significant increase in heat generation, making its management crucial for business efficiency. Data centers can produce heat densities ranging from 50 watts to as high as 300 watts per square foot. Therefore, cooling has become an important and costly issue, accounting for up to 40 percent of total energy consumption in data centers.

This has propelled the development of new and emerging cooling technologies, as traditional cooling methods such as air cooling, refrigeration and “free” cooling—although effective to a degree—have presented challenges such as high energy consumption, limited scalability, significant environmental impact and high operational cost. To that end, trends in the evolution of cooling technology have emerged to combat these challenges, including direct-to-chip cooling, liquid cooling and reliance on renewable source energy. In particular, liquid immersion cooling, which involves the submergence of electronic components (such as servers and other IT hardware) into a thermally conductive but electrically insulating liquid, continues to gain traction in the industry. Each of these technologies provide specific benefits and may be better suited to different data centers depending on the facility’s characteristics. With these advances in cooling technologies, data center operators may find themselves in a conundrum when navigating between the various options.

This article outlines some key considerations for data center operators to consider when determining how to cool their data center.

## 1. Assess the business drivers and data center’s operational characteristics

When considering cooling technologies, it is essential to first assess the specific business drivers and operational characteristics of the data center facility, whether existing or greenfield. A comprehensive review of the financial, operational and other critical business drivers should help an operator identify the essential characteristics required of any potential cooling technology option. For example, if retrofitting an existing data center, it will be important to consider the existing cooling mechanisms and their compatibility with alternate cooling methods in tandem with the broader, strategic business goals.

On the other hand, a new facility should incorporate a design that can scale over time, not just regarding cooling but for all critical equipment, including power and security. This approach could help to deliver both a first mover advantage and competitive differentiation, and, in turn, position a business to stay ahead of changes to regulatory requirements. For example, the American Innovation & Manufacturing (AIM) Act, enacted in December 2020, mandates an ongoing gradual phase-down of the production and consumption of hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), which are widely used as refrigerants in cooling systems. This has resulted in increased costs for maintenance and refrigerant replacement as HFC supplies diminish and prices rise. By proactively implementing cutting-edge cooling solutions as part of its ongoing operational strategy, operators can ensure compliance with future regulations, thereby reducing the risk of potential disruptions and compliance costs, and set itself apart as an industry leader in innovation and environmental responsibility.

## 2. Conduct an energy and cost analysis

Another key consideration is to perform an energy and cost analysis to understand the actual costs of a particular cooling technology. Typically, data center operators will find tension between the long-term sustainability and efficiency of modern cooling technologies, and the significant initial capital expenditure to acquire and install them. Costs associated with cooling technologies often extend well beyond the initial acquisition and installation to long-term operational, maintenance and repair costs. For example, liquid immersion technology involves high acquisition costs, complex installation and large floor space requirements, all of which may not be suitable for small, high-density data centers with more pronounced space and budget constraints. For remote facilities, experimental cooling technologies may not be appropriate, as the cost of building and maintaining infrastructure may be both logistically challenging and prohibitively costly. Water-based cooling technologies may also not be appropriate for facilities located in desert regions. However, the abundance of sunlight in those locations may be leveraged to generate solar power—traditional energy may prove to be more effective in such areas.

## 3. Engage in collaborative design for new data centers

Given the aforementioned tensions, it is important for operators to adopt a collaborative design approach in determining how cooling considerations may be integrated into the design of the facility.  Greenfield developments have a distinct advantage here. Raised floors, vents and different floor configurations allow for airflow optimization. The use of reflective materials or coatings on the roof of the facility may also significantly reduce heat absorption. Free cooling is significantly more effective in warmer regions. Thus, for data centers in the desert, it may be helpful to include shading devices, insulation, vents, windows and other natural ventilation mechanisms in the design. This process may also result in hybrid cooling systems by using multiple cooling technologies, which can create a robust system with backups if one cooling system malfunctions.

## 4. Evaluate vendors

At the commercial level, data center operators should also conduct a thorough vendor evaluation as part of selecting their cooling technology. Given the immense business impact, the choice of a vendor should not be based solely on cost. Perhaps, more importantly, operators should focus on reliability, sustainability and innovation. This may be done through a competitive tender process that clearly outlines all the energy, scalability and other critical business requirements specific to the operation.

## 5. Utilize advanced technologies

Increasingly, the use of systems that allow an operator to leverage data and artificial intelligence to optimize and operate cooling technology for their facilities has become an important part of the overall commercial consideration. For example, the implementation of real-time monitoring systems provides insight into the actual cooling needs of the facility, thereby providing valuable data for an operator to evaluate the efficiency of different cooling methods. These information systems can also provide up-to-date and accurate information on minor temperature changes, alerting an operator to a system malfunction or an environmental anomaly, which can prove to be vital to long-term maintenance and repair decisions.

Evaluating the benefits and challenges of various cooling technologies in relation to the data center’s specific business and operational demands is the most effective approach to selecting the optimal cooling technology. Emerging technologies continue to develop in this area, as evidenced by experimental proposals on building data centers underwater and in space. In resolving most challenges, there is no one-size-fits-all approach, and operators should focus on what is most appropriate for their specific priorities. Regardless of what they may be, scalability, sustainability and security will likely continue to be the key principles driving decisions on this issue.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative cooling methods, including liquid cooling and immersion systems, enhance efficiency, reduce energy consumption, and address high-density workloads in modern data centers.
Emerging hardware solutions, such as AI-optimized processors and low-power components, significantly decrease energy usage while maintaining high performance for data center operations.
IoT devices enable real-time monitoring of temperature and humidity, allowing data centers to optimize cooling systems, prevent overheating, and enhance overall operational efficiency.

enriched_points.point_title: Future of AI in Data Centers
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI technologies will drive predictive analytics for cooling demands, automate management systems, and improve energy efficiency, thus reducing operational costs in data centers.
